parameters.content:
### Daily Schedule

Starts the workflow on a timer ‚Äî daily at 7:00. No input data; downstream nodes fetch what they need.

Next: Required Fields Present?

### Required Fields Present?

Two-way gate on `={{ $json.from }}`. TRUE continues the happy path, FALSE takes the alternate branch.

Ports: ‚ë†‚Üí Slot Free? ¬∑ ‚ë°‚Üí Abort ‚Äî Malformed Input

### Abort ‚Äî Malformed Input

Fails fast with a clear message; triggers the error workflow instead of processing bad data.

### Slot Free?

Two-way gate on `={{ $items().length === 0 || !$json.id }}`. TRUE continues the happy path, FALSE takes the alternate branch.

Ports: ‚ë†‚Üí Record Booking ¬∑ ‚ë°‚Üí Propose Alternative Slots

### Record Booking

Runs a parameterized SQL statement against Postgres ‚Äî values are bound via `queryReplacement`, not string-interpolated, so it's immune to SQL injection.

Ports: ‚ë†‚Üí Send Confirmation (Slack) ¬∑ ‚ë°‚Üí Capture Failure Context

‚Üª retries 3√ó/2s ¬∑ ‚ö† failures exit via the error rail ¬∑ üîë Postgres connection

### Send Confirmation (Slack)

Posts a message to Slack (`#inbox-ops`) ‚Äî a low-latency way for the team to see automation activity without checking a dashboard.

Ports: ‚ë†‚Üí Hold Until Reminder Time ¬∑ ‚ë°‚Üí Capture Failure Context

‚Üª retries 3√ó/2s ¬∑ ‚ö† failures exit via the error rail ¬∑ üîë Slack OAuth2

### Propose Alternative Slots

Posts a message to Slack (`#inbox-ops`) ‚Äî a low-latency way for the team to see automation activity without checking a dashboard.

‚Üª retries 3√ó/2s ¬∑ ‚ö† failures exit via the error rail ¬∑ üîë Slack OAuth2

### Hold Until Reminder Time

Pauses 1 hours ‚Äî spacing calls to respect rate limits.

Next: Send Reminder (Slack)

### Send Reminder (Slack)

Posts a message to Slack (`#inbox-ops`) ‚Äî a low-latency way for the team to see automation activity without checking a dashboard.

Ports: ‚ë†‚Üí Sort Emails Newest First ¬∑ ‚ë°‚Üí Capture Failure Context

‚Üª retries 3√ó/2s ¬∑ ‚ö† failures exit via the error rail ¬∑ üîë Slack OAuth2

### Sort Emails Newest First

Orders items (newest first) so limits and digests pick the most relevant records.

Next: Send Summary to Slack

### Send Summary to Slack

Posts a message to Slack (`#inbox-ops`) ‚Äî a low-latency way for the team to see automation activity without checking a dashboard.

‚Üª retries 3√ó/2s ¬∑ ‚ö† failures exit via the error rail ¬∑ üîë Slack OAuth2

### Capture Failure Context

Shapes the item: writes `failedNode`, `errorMessage`, `workflow`. Keeps the schema explicit between stages.

Next: Escalate Failure to Slack

### Escalate Failure to Slack

Posts a message to Slack (`#inbox-ops`) ‚Äî a low-latency way for the team to see automation activity without checking a dashboard.

# Remind open deals with PostgreSQL and Slack for your team

**Pattern:** Reminders & Follow-ups ¬∑ **Level:** Core ¬∑ **Trigger:** Scheduled

## What it does
Remind open deals with PostgreSQL and Slack for your team. It runs automatically on a schedule.

## Why it exists
Manual auto-responders is slow and error-prone; this workflow makes the process repeatable, monitored and hands-off, with failures surfaced instead of silently lost.

## Main flow
Daily Schedule ‚Üí Required Fields Present? ‚Üí Slot Free? ‚Üí Record Booking ‚Üí Send Confirmation (Slack) ‚Üí Hold Until Reminder Time ‚Üí Send Reminder (Slack) ‚Üí ‚Ä¶

## Use it for
- Teams that want to reminders & follow-ups without manual effort
- Anyone automating auto-responders in marketing
- Replacing a fragile manual process with a reliable workflow

## You get
- Has retries and error handling built in
- Runs 24/7 in the background
- Keeps your systems in sync

## Before first run
Connect credentials: Postgres connection; Slack OAuth2.
Follow the color key: üü¶ entry ¬∑ üü™ AI ¬∑ üü´ actions ¬∑ ‚¨ú utilities ¬∑ üü© outputs ¬∑ üü• error rail.
Each node has its own doc card underneath ‚Äî read those before editing.

## Try it now
Sample data is pinned on the trigger ‚Äî hit **Execute Workflow** to watch it flow through every node before connecting any real credentials. Unpin the trigger (right-click ‚Üí Unpin) when you're ready to go live.

## Design decisions

Availability is checked *before* booking, not assumed ‚Äî creating an event unconditionally would double-book the same slot if two requests arrive close together. The confirmation and the later reminder are separate sends because they serve different moments: confirming intent now, and cutting no-shows right before the appointment.

## Practice exercises

Once it's wired up with real credentials, try:

1. Change the reminder lead time from 1 hour to 24 hours.
2. Simulate two near-simultaneous booking requests for the same slot and see which gets the alternative-slots branch.
3. Add a cancellation path that frees the slot back up.

## üõü Error handling & retries

External calls retry **3√ó with 2s backoff**. Anything still failing exits through its node's error output into the red error rail ‚Üí context is captured and the on-call channel is alerted. Set an n8n **Error Workflow** in settings as the final safety net.

## ‚è± Rate limiting

Work is batched and spaced with Wait so provider quotas aren't tripped. If you hit 429s, lower the batch size or raise the wait ‚Äî throughput drops, reliability rises.

## üõ† Customize me

Quick wins: point the data nodes at your own accounts (see üîë lines on each card), adjust the trigger cadence, and tune thresholds/labels to your auto-responders process. Every node card explains its role ‚Äî safe to extend at any marked branch.
